reports.title: HDDs - The Next Leg Higher
reports.headline: MS raises STX/WDC PTs and out-year EPS materially as Asia checks point to HDD shortages through CY28 and stronger nearline $/TB pricing.
reports.summary: Morgan Stanley argues the HDD cycle is extending as cloud demand and AI inferencing/agents broaden nearline HDD demand while supply remains constrained, driving shortages through at least CY28 and materially better pricing power. Asia checks suggest demand is growing 40-50% annually vs supply EB growth of 30-35%, with vendors targeting $25-30/TB in CY27/CY28 vs current blended nearline pricing below $15/TB. MS raises STX and WDC FY27/FY28 EPS and PTs, keeps both OW, and retains STX as Top Pick for NT GM upside, while noting WDC may screen better LT on bull-case upside and lower lev.
important_observations: Asia checks imply nearline HDD supply shortfalls of ~300EB in CY26 and ~400EB in both CY27 and CY28, extending the cycle beyond prior expectations.
HDD vendors appear to have materially more pricing power, targeting $25-30/TB in CY27/CY28 while MS base case still assumes <$20/TB in CY27 and ~$22/TB in CY28.
Hyperscalers are reportedly deploying HDDs near 100% rate vs ~70% historically, suggesting limited CSP inventory build despite strong demand.
ODMs reportedly hold only 1-2 weeks of HDD inventory, supporting the shortage thesis.
MS sees incremental HDD pricing upside as highly accretive because incremental price largely flows through to profit after tax.
STX remains tactical Top Pick due to Sep-26 HAMR repricing opportunity and faster NT cost-down potential; WDC has more LT bull-case upside and less lev.
new_data: Asia checks over the last 3 weeks, including Taiwan AI Summit, COMPUTEX, and WDC Asia roadshow, indicate strengthening HDD demand and pricing.
Nearline supply shortfall now expected at ~300EB in CY26 and ~400EB in CY27/CY28.
Current blended nearline price/TB for STX/WDC is $14.30-14.90; vendor targets are now up to $25-30/TB over the next 2-3 years.
Spot HDD prices are reportedly ~30% above prior captured pricing, with some distributor sales at $30-35/TB.
Hyperscalers are asking for LTAs out to 2032, but vendors are limiting 12+ month commercial commitments to preserve pricing flexibility.
management_insights: WDC mgmt framed new entrant risk as more constrained by technology qualification, vertical integration, and limited supply-chain capacity than by capital availability.
WDC mgmt noted HAMR qualification took more than 10 years, underscoring barriers to entry.
Mgmt commentary suggested Chinese cold-storage alternatives such as Huawei's Magneto-Electric Disk have had limited success.
Vendors want to provide customers ~5 years of capacity/technology visibility but are less willing to lock in commercial terms beyond 12 months.
estimate_changes: STX PT raised to $1,035 from $767; WDC PT raised to $650 from $488.
STX FY27 EPS raised 11% to $35.38 and FY28 EPS raised ~31% to $70.64; FY26 EPS unchanged.
WDC FY27 EPS raised 13% to $22.40 and FY28 EPS raised 25% to $43.47; FY26 EPS unchanged.
Changes are driven mainly by higher nearline price/TB, with no material changes to EB growth, cost declines, or opex.
MS FY28 EPS now sits ~70% above cons for both STX and WDC.

reports.title: TSMC: Domination in leading-edge foundry continues; OW
reports.headline: MS stays OW and raises PT to NT$2,888 on stronger 2026 rev/capex outlook, 2nm demand, and leading-edge pricing power.
reports.summary: MS expects TSMC to raise 2026 rev guide to near +40% y/y and capex to US$56bn on strong AI chip demand across CPU/GPU/ASIC/optical networking. The note argues Samsung/Intel foundry competition remains minor given TSMC's scale, EUV allocation, and process leadership, with potential 5%-10% leading-edge wafer price hikes in 2027. PT moves to NT$2,888 from NT$2,588 and EPS ests rise for 2026-28.
important_observations: 2nm demand appears materially stronger than prior plan, with fab floorplan changes implying +60kwpm vs a year-ago plan.
AI CPU, GPU, ASIC, and networking are cited as the main drivers of incremental leading-edge demand.
TSMC may retain strong pricing power despite second-source efforts by US customers, given capacity catch-up and node leadership.
TSMC 2nm+3nm capacity could reach ~400k wpm by EoY 2028, roughly 10x Intel 14A+18A and Samsung SF2 scale cited in the note.
Potential 2027 leading-edge wafer price hikes of 5%-10% are framed as achievable if capacity and EUV advantages persist.
new_data: Expected 2026 rev guide raise to close to +40% y/y growth.
Expected 2026 capex guide raise to US$56bn, high end of the initial US$52-56bn range.
Industry checks suggest TSMC changed parts of 3nm fab floorplan to 2nm, implying 2nm demand +60kwpm above prior plan.
2027 logic-foundry EUV allocation cited at 40+ units for TSMC vs 9-11 for Intel and 5-7 for Samsung foundry.
Speculative possibility Intel outsources some 2nm server CPU to TSMC from 2028.
management_insights: Key 2Q26 call questions include competitive response to Samsung/Intel, 2027 wafer pricing, memory-cost effects on foundry orders, and advanced-packaging utilization.
No direct mgmt commentary included; call preview is based on analyst expectations and checks.
estimate_changes: PT raised to NT$2,888 from NT$2,588.
2026 EPS raised to NT$107.56 from NT$103.34.
2027 EPS raised to NT$143.01 from NT$128.97.
2028 EPS raised to NT$177.30 from NT$158.42.

reports.title: Amazon Advertising: Third Largest Digital Ad Platform, High Growth/High Margin, & Beneficiary of AI/Agentic
reports.headline: JPM views AMZN Ads as a high-growth/high-margin business with limited NT AI platform risk and keeps AMZN as a best idea.
reports.summary: JPM argues AMZN has become the #3 scaled global digital ad platform with ~9% share and a large remaining retail media opportunity. The note frames Ads as a major margin tailwind, with 2026E ad rev of ~$83B growing +21% FXN y/y and ~$38B of OI at ~46% OM, equal to ~36% of total OI. JPM sees AI/ML improving ranking, retrieval, DSP performance, ROAS, and conversions, while OpenAI/agentic ad risk is viewed as low NT because commercial intent, ad tech maturity, and measurement remain weaker than core channels.
important_observations: Retail media is one of the fastest-growing online ad verticals and is estimated at ~20% of US online ad spend.
AMZN is positioned as the third scaled digital ad platform after GOOG/L and META, with ~9% global digital ad share.
Ads are becoming a material profit pool, with JPM estimating ~$38B of 2026E OI from advertising, ~36% of AMZN total OI.
Prime Video is highlighted as a major emerging ad surface with 315M+ global MAUs and potential $7B-$8B annualized 2026 ad rev.
OpenAI ads are viewed as more likely to pull experimental budgets initially than sustained high-ROAS spend from AMZN/GOOG/META.
new_data: 2026E AMZN Advertising rev estimated at ~$83B, +21% FXN y/y.
2026E Ads OM estimated at ~46%, implying ~$38B OI.
Core Sponsored ads estimated at ~85% of AMZN Advertising Services.
Prime Video estimated at $7B-$8B annualized 2026 ad rev, or ~8-10% of 2026E ad rev.
OpenAI press reports cited for ad rev expectations of ~$2.5B in 2026, $11B in 2027, and $100B+ by 2030.
estimate_changes: No explicit estimate revisions provided; current EPS fcsts and PT are shown without prior values.

estimate_change_details.note: Current PT disclosed; no prior PT shown, so pct change cannot be computed.

evidence_quality: Analyst model work: AMZN Ads rev/OI, Prime Video ad rev, and EPS/PT are JPM estimates, so weight depends on model assumptions.
Market/industry data: TAM/share claims use online ad market sizing and retail media mix estimates; useful for framing but not company-reported.
Company/platform data: Prime Video MAUs and ad surface discussion appear grounded in platform disclosures/observable product suite, but exact monetization split is estimated.

reports.title: TPG Inc. (TPG): Deep dive reinforces peer-leading growth outlook; Mgmt meeting highlights
reports.headline: Mgmt mtg reinforced GS view that TPG has peer-leading FRE and DE/share growth, supported by fundraising, Wealth, Credit, Real Estate, and margin expansion.
reports.summary: GS hosted mgmt mtgs with TPG CEO Jon Winkelried and came away more confident in TPG’s multi-year earnings algorithm, with 2025-28E FRE and after-tax DE/share CAGRs of 22%, above cons by 9% and 7%, respectively. Mgmt said TPG remains on track for $50bn+ of 2026 fundraising, sees tailwinds into 2027-28 from Real Estate, Credit, Wealth and smaller initiatives, and reaffirmed a balance-sheet-light model despite CFO Axel André’s insurance background. GS frames valuation as disconnected from growth, with TPG trading below balance-sheet-light peer avgs and PT implying >60% upside.
important_observations: TPG’s organic mgmt fee growth is expected to inflect to 16% CAGR from 2Q26-4Q28E vs 9% pro forma since 2022.
$80bn flagship fundraising cycle spans 20+ funds across all six platforms, excluding SMAs and new product initiatives.
Wealth channel is scaling, with retail fundraising expected to rise from ~5% to ~13% of firmwide FRR.
Credit deployment of shadow AUM is expected to be fee-rate accretive and support mgmt fee growth.
FRE margin expected to recover above 50% by 2028E.
CFO transition moves Jack Weingart fully to CEO of Global Wealth Solutions and appoints Axel André as CFO; mgmt emphasized no shift away from balance-sheet-light strategy.

GS deep dive points to 15% 2025-28E mgmt fee CAGR, 22% FRE CAGR, and 22% DE/share CAGR.
GS estimates 16% organic mgmt fee CAGR for 2Q26-4Q28E.
Stock cited at 15.7x 2027E P/FRE and 13.6x 2027E P/DE vs peer avgs of 18.7x and 15.9x.
management_insights: Mgmt highlighted differentiated returns and DPI in PE as support for 2026 fundraising.
Mgmt sees further 2027-28 tailwinds from Real Estate, Credit, Wealth and smaller initiatives.
Wealth traction includes steady retail PE contributions, Credit share gains, and upcoming new product launches.
Mgmt reaffirmed commitment to a balance-sheet-light model while pursuing incremental insurance partnerships.

reports.title: Guidewire Software: Takeaways from Fireside Chat with Management
reports.headline: Mgmt sees durable upper-teens ARR growth supported by accel P&C modernization demand, AI urgency, and broader cloud platform TAM, while qtr bookings timing and contract mechanics remain key watch items.
reports.summary: JPM hosted GWRE mgmt for a fireside chat focused on P&C core modernization, ARR momentum, cloud platform strategy, AI modules, intl localization, implementation capacity, and contract mechanics. Mgmt highlighted rising modernization urgency as insurers need modern core rails for AI/agentic workflows, reiterated confidence in durable upper-teens ARR growth into FY27 without formal guide, and framed AI-enabled modules such as ProNavigator, UnderwritingCenter, and PricingCenter as the next TAM expansion lever. Contract-defined renewals and normal bookings seasonality limit simple step-up narratives and keep qtr timing noisy.
important_observations: AI is increasing urgency for P&C core modernization because legacy mainframe/fragmented stacks make agentic workflows difficult to build and scale.
Mgmt confidence in durable upper-teens ARR growth appears tied to broad market momentum rather than a formal FY27 guide.
ARR quality is being supported by larger fully ramped ARR commitments plus improving churn/retention dynamics.
Bookings timing remains noisy given large project sizes and board/signature processes, with normal seasonality skewed to 4Q and deal slippage across qtrs.
Renewal upside is constrained by contract-defined pricing; GWRE cannot simply reset early cloud customers to list pricing at renewal.
AI modules and agentic framework are positioned to expand GWRE beyond the original cloud transition thesis, but monetization and pricing are still in discovery.
new_data: Mgmt linked FY27 ARR confidence to durable upper-teens growth expectation, while explicitly not providing formal guide.
GWRE described DWP-based pricing mechanics, including true-ups in inflationary environments and renewal constraints.
Mgmt discussed improving implementation capacity through SI ecosystem enablement, more self-service, and AI-aided implementations.
Intl localization friction is reportedly declining due to regulatory content abstraction work.
management_insights: P&C insurers still have meaningful exposure to mainframe and fragmented core stacks, supporting modernization demand.
Mgmt sees open core rails as critical so Guidewire Cloud Platform does not constrain insurer AI strategies.
ProNavigator is currently user-in-the-loop, with potential evolution toward productivity gains and eventual automation as trust/regulatory constraints ease.
UnderwritingCenter is intended to improve submission triage/workflow while retaining human approval loops where required.
PricingCenter and ProNavigator could become meaningful ACV attach opportunities to core modules.
evidence_quality: Management meeting/call: primary sourcing from CFO and VP IR fireside chat; useful for strategy, demand tone, and contract mechanics.
Company-reported commentary/guidance: ARR growth discussion is mgmt outlook commentary, not formal FY27 guide, so weight as directional.
Analyst inference: TAM expansion, AI monetization potential, and ARR quality framing rely partly on JPM synthesis of mgmt comments.
No channel checks/survey/proprietary dataset cited; limited independent triangulation in the excerpt.
risks_or_watch_items: Qtr bookings volatility from large-deal timing, approvals, and seasonality.
Contract-defined renewals may cap upside from repricing early cloud cohorts.
AI module monetization remains evolving with ongoing price discovery.
Trust/regulatory constraints could slow automation adoption in underwriting and claims workflows.
Implementation capacity and intl localization remain execution items despite noted improvements.
key_numbers: ARR outlook: durable upper-teens growth expectation into FY27, not formal guide

reports.title: Micron Technology: Substantial Beat-and-Raise and Major SCA Expansion Anchor a Higher and More Durable Earnings Floor; Capital Return Pivot Post-Dec 2026 Adds Another Value Unlock Lever; Reit OW
reports.headline: MU beat/raised materially, expanded SCAs to 16 agreements, and JPM lifted Dec-27 PT to $1,540 while reit OW.
reports.summary: JPM argues MU’s SCA expansion is the bigger development than the large May-qtr beat/raise, as contracted volume, floor pricing, and customer deposits may structurally reduce cyclicality and create a higher rev/GM floor. May-qtr rev, ASPs, and GM were well ahead of cons, Aug-qtr guide implies another step-up, and mgmt now sees memory tightness persisting beyond CY27. JPM raised FY26 EPS ests materially and sees a capital return inflection after Dec 2026 as cash and FCF build.
important_observations: SCAs expanded from 1 contract to 16 signed agreements, potentially shifting MU from cyclical commodity producer toward multi-year contracted supplier.
SCAs cover ~20% of DRAM volume and ~33% of NAND volume across CY26-CY30, with mgmt targeting 50%+ of co rev under commitments once planned agreements are executed.
14 SCAs with price bands represent ~$100B cumulative rev at floor pricing, with floor-price GM described as well above prior cycle peak margins.
Customer deposits/financial commitments of $22B, including $18B unrestricted cash, strengthen B/S and signal customer commitment.
Capital return could inflect after Dec 9, 2026 CHIPS Act anniversary, with mgmt indicating 100% return of excess cash over time, mainly via buybacks.
new_data: May-qtr rev $41.5B, +74% q/q and +346% y/y, above Street cons of $35.6B.
May-qtr GM reached 84.9%, an all-time high.
DRAM ASP +63% q/q and NAND ASP +87% q/q per JPMe.
Aug-qtr guide: rev ~$50B, GM ~86%, EPS ~$31.
HBM TAM for CY27 raised to easily above $100B vs prior CY28 timing.
management_insights: Mgmt guided to meaningful moderation in rate of price increases, but JPM emphasizes durability of current price levels under SCAs.
Mgmt expects DRAM/NAND supply-demand tightness to persist beyond CY27 vs prior beyond CY26.
Mgmt said floor-price GMs under SCAs are well above peak margins in any prior cycle.
Mgmt expects FY26 capex ~$27B and FY27 above mid-$40B level to support CY28+ supply additions.
Mgmt explicitly indicated excess cash should be returned to shareholders over time, with buybacks primary.
estimate_changes: FY26 adj EPS raised 25% to $73.69 from $58.84.
Dec-27 PT established at $1,540 vs prior Dec-26 PT of $550.

PT basis rolled/raised from prior Dec-26 PT to Dec-27 PT.

Management call/commentary: primary but mgmt-framed evidence on SCAs, pricing floors, capex, supply tightness, and capital return, high relevance but execution-dependent.
Analyst model work: JPM est/PT changes and ASP calculations are analyst-derived; useful but sensitive to pricing and margin assumptions.

reports.title: TSMC: Assessing Competition Risk for Foundry and Advanced Packaging
reports.headline: JPM argues Intel/Samsung competition risk is overstated; TSMC retains a 2+ yr leading-edge process lead and demand shortage/geopolitics are driving customer diversification talk.
reports.summary: JPM frames recent investor concern over higher foundry/advanced packaging competition as mainly a byproduct of severe leading-edge capacity shortages and geopolitics rather than a structural erosion of TSMC’s moat. The note argues TSMC remains well ahead on process tech, with Intel 18A closer to TSMC N3 and Intel 14A unlikely to ramp meaningfully until TSMC’s N2 family is already at large scale. Cost is not seen as the driver for diversification because yield-adjusted chip cost remains lowest at TSMC despite higher wafer prices. JPM maintains OW with Dec-26 PT of NT$2,500.
important_observations: Investor debate has shifted to whether Intel Foundry and other competitors can cause a TSMC de-rating in leading-edge foundry and advanced backend.
JPM sees the competition narrative as cyclical and similar to prior late-upcycle concerns around Samsung 3nm and Intel 20A/18A.
Leading-edge demand/supply gap is described as historically wide, especially at N3, incentivizing fabless customers to seek supplemental capacity even if ramp success odds are low.
Geopolitics are pushing some US fabless vendors to engage Intel Foundry for local manufacturing optionality, not because Intel has better economics.
TSMC’s DTCO/co-design capabilities are presented as an underappreciated source of PPA advantage beyond simple public node-spec comparisons.
new_data: JPM estimates the 2026 N3 demand-supply gap at roughly 600k wafers.
JPM estimates the semi upcycle has reached 32 consecutive months of y/y growth.
JPM benchmarks Intel 18A at roughly TSMC N3 baseline, and Intel 18AP at roughly TSMC N3X.
JPM expects Intel 14A risk production in 2028 and HVM in 2029.
JPM expects TSMC N2 family to reach a $50-60bn rev run-rate by the time Intel 14A ramps.

estimate_change_details.note: PT disclosed, but no prior PT provided in extracted text.

evidence_quality: Analyst model work: node benchmarking, N3 wafer gap, and N2 rev run-rate are JPM estimates; useful but dependent on assumptions.
Market/industry data: semi upcycle duration and capacity-shortage framing support the cyclical-demand argument; appears broad but not fully sourced in excerpt.
Customer/channel feedback: process PPA and wafer-price benchmarking references customer feedback; likely triangulated but detail is limited in excerpt.
Analyst inference: conclusion that diversification is driven by shortage/geopolitics rather than cost is reasoned from yield-adjusted cost and historical cycles.
risks_or_watch_items: Intel 18A/18AP customer wins and actual yield/ramp progress could change investor perception before meaningful rev share shift occurs.
TSMC N3/N2 capacity additions and customer allocation outcomes remain key NT catalysts.
US localization pressure could support more dual-sourcing even if economics favor TSMC.
Any evidence of Intel 14A pulling in schedule or achieving strong PPA/yield could challenge JPM’s 2+ yr lead view.
Advanced packaging competition risk is flagged in the title, but the provided excerpt contains limited detail on backend-specific conclusions.
key_numbers: Semi upcycle duration: 32 months of consecutive y/y growth
Expected TSMC N2 family rev run-rate by Intel 14A ramp: $50-60bn

reports.companies_covered: ARDT, HCA, THC, UHS, CI, CVS, CNC, ELV, HUM, MOH, UNH
reports.headline: Expert call suggests Q2 hospital trends below plan on weaker outpatient surgery and worse payer mix; neg hospitals, pos MCOs.
reports.summary: Mizuho hosted senior execs from two not-for-profit hospital systems and came away more cautious on hospital Q2 trends: Apr/May ops were below internal budgets, outpatient surgery weakness persisted into Jun, and payer mix/collectability deteriorated more than expected from eAPTC/HIX dynamics and rising patient responsibility. Inpatient vols improved in early Jun, especially at the TX system, but ED/observation-driven lower-acuity mix is pressuring rev/admission and margins. The note frames the data point as neg for hospitals ARDT/HCA/THC/UHS and pos for managed care CI/CVS/CNC/ELV/HUM/MOH/UNH.
important_observations: Both systems are tracking below annual budgets, with weak Apr/May unlikely to be fully offset by stronger Jun inpatient vols.
Outpatient surgery weakness is continuing into Jun despite inpatient improvement, raising concern elective demand may be softening from consumer affordability pressure.
ED-driven admissions and elevated observation stays are supporting volume but worsening acuity, throughput, reimbursement, and rev quality.
Payer mix deterioration appears worse than expected, with higher self-pay-like exposure, slower collections, A/R aging, bad debt, and reserve builds.
Cost inflation is not the main problem; margin pressure is more from payer mix, collectability, and lower-quality volume conversion.
Call is a neg read-through for hospital operators and a pos read-through for MCOs if hospital utilization/reimbursement quality remains pressured.
new_data: TX inpatient admits +3% y/y in Apr/May vs +4-5% expected, accelerating to +9% in first 2 wks of Jun.
TX inpatient surgery flat in Apr, +4% y/y in May, and +20% y/y in early Jun.
Mountain West admits flat-to-down in Apr/May and approx -2% YTD; inpatient surgery flat Apr and +2% y/y May.
TX outpatient surgery +4% y/y Apr, -4% May, and -4% early Jun; Mountain West outpatient surgery -3% y/y in May.
Mountain West ASC vols grew approx +12% y/y, highlighting continued migration away from hospital-based elective procedures.
Occupancy remains tight at roughly 85-90%, while TX observation LOS remains elevated at roughly 40-50 hours.
management_insights: Hospital execs attributed mix pressure to eAPTC expiration, worse HIX mix, higher Bronze-plan enrollment, and affordability pressure.
Mgmt indicated outpatient procedural weakness reflects elective softness, physician availability, scheduling delays, and capacity constraints.
Both systems flagged rising patient responsibility balances, slower collections, and more A/R aging into 90-120 day buckets.
TX highlighted 340B uncertainty and pricing/manufacturer pressure as an added profitability watch item.
Mgmt expects vol strength to continue but margin recovery to remain limited until HIX/self-pay trends stabilize.
evidence_quality: Expert call / mgmt commentary: primary anecdotal input from senior execs at two not-for-profit hospital systems; useful QTD color but limited sample size.
Company operating data: system-level volume, mix, margin, occupancy, A/R, and cost datapoints; high relevance but not public-company reported results.
Triangulation: compares two regions, TX and Mountain West, with overlapping payer-mix and outpatient surgery themes, improving read-through confidence.
Analyst inference: public-company implications for hospitals and MCOs are inferred from not-for-profit system trends, not direct ARDT/HCA/THC/UHS/managed-care disclosures.
risks_or_watch_items: Whether Jun inpatient rebound sustains and offsets Apr/May weakness.
Whether outpatient surgery weakness proves temporary timing/capacity noise or broader elective-demand softness.
Persistence of self-pay/HIX/Bronze-plan mix pressure after eAPTC expiration.
Bad debt, reserve builds, A/R aging, and realized pricing into Q2/Q3 hospital earnings.
Observation volumes and LOS crowding out higher-acuity admissions and elective throughput.
key_numbers: TX admits +3% y/y Apr/May vs +4-5% expected; +9% y/y early Jun.
TX inpatient surgery flat Apr, +4% y/y May, +20% y/y early Jun.
Mountain West admits flat-to-down Apr/May and approx -2% YTD; inpatient surgery +2% y/y May.
TX outpatient surgery +4% Apr, -4% May, -4% early Jun; Mountain West outpatient surgery -3% May.
Occupancy roughly 85-90%; TX observation LOS roughly 40-50 hrs.
Mountain West net rev/admission down approx $200 y/y.
TX Medicare rev mix approx 48% to 47.5%; Medicaid 8.1% to 7.8%; exchange 10.7% to 10.0% YTD.
TX self-pay approx 8.4% historically, 9.2% YTD, 10.8% Apr, 10.5% May.
TX Q2 margin approx 2% vs 5% plan; Mountain West mid-single-digit EBITDA shortfall YTD.

reports.title: Chipflation – Navigating A Memory Crisis
reports.companies_covered: MU, SNDK, KIOXIA, STX, WDC, ASML, AMAT, KLAC
reports.headline: AI-driven memory scarcity is shifting pricing power to memory/HDD/semi-cap suppliers while pressuring downstream OEM margins and device affordability.
reports.summary: MS argues AI demand has turned memory into a structural bottleneck across HBM, DRAM and enterprise SSDs, with prices up ~6x in the past year and supply additions taking years to build/qualify/ramp. The note frames this as a durable supply-demand reset rather than a normal semi cycle, with LTAs/prepayments reallocating capacity toward hyperscalers and AI buyers while consumer PC/smartphone/autos/networking/industrial OEMs face tighter allocation, higher COGS, spec cuts, launch delays and potential demand destruction.
important_observations: Memory market behavior is moving from commodity pricing toward allocation via LTAs, prepayments and strategic supply commitments.
HBM is crowding out conventional DRAM capacity, creating spillover tightness in server DRAM and enterprise SSDs and reducing availability for non-AI end markets.
MS sees smartphone/PC memory supply falling 12-15% short despite total DRAM wafer capacity expanding ~30% by 2027.
Large cloud buyers are better positioned to secure supply and capitalize higher costs; consumer-exposed OEMs with weaker pricing power face the sharpest margin risk.
Macro impact may be more visible in PPI, corporate margins, cloud bills, capex budgets and delayed tech deployment than in headline CPI.
new_data: Memory prices up ~6x over the past year across key categories tied to AI demand.
Total DRAM wafer capacity expected to expand ~30% by 2027, but mix shift still leaves smartphone/PC memory supply 12-15% short.
US/China policy tools could help LT supply but are unlikely to relieve NT tightness given build/qualification/ramp timelines.
evidence_quality: Market/industry data: primary/market-based pricing and supply-demand evidence; high relevance for cycle framing, though detailed data tables are not included in excerpt.
Analyst model work: MS capacity and shortage fcsts underpin the 2027 DRAM and smartphone/PC supply-gap claims; useful but dependent on mix and demand assumptions.
Policy analysis: mostly analyst/economist inference on US/CN supply response timing and restrictions; lower precision for investment timing.
Cross-sector equity implications: analyst inference based on cost exposure and pricing power; directionally useful, less company-specific without est revisions.
risks_or_watch_items: HBM capacity additions or faster qualification could ease scarcity sooner than expected.
Demand destruction in consumer devices could reduce conventional memory demand and soften pricing.
Policy subsidies, tax credits or permitting reform may alter LT supply response but likely with multi-year lag.
OEM ability to pass through costs, redesign specs or delay launches will determine margin damage by company.
Watch LTAs/prepayments and hyperscaler capex commitments as indicators of sustained allocation pressure.
key_numbers: Memory prices up ~6x in past year
Smartphone/PC memory supply 12-15% short despite capacity expansion

reports.title: Making the Case for Another Leg Higher in the Memory Trade: Performing in a Contract Year; U/G SNDK to OW, PT to $2,300
reports.headline: Barclays argues memory/storage contracts are structurally improving visibility and pricing power, upgrades SNDK to OW, and raises PTs for MU, SNDK, STX, and WDC.
reports.summary: Barclays frames memory/storage as the most attractive vertical below accelerators, citing persistent supply/demand imbalance through CY27 and a shift toward LTAs/NBMs/SCAs with pricing floors, volume commitments, financial guarantees, and in some cases prepayments. The note’s variant view is that these mechanisms can reduce commodity cyclicality, support higher multiples, and create another leg higher for SNDK, MU, STX, and WDC, with SNDK upgraded to OW and PTs raised across the group.
important_observations: Contract structures are the core thesis: LTAs/NBMs/SCAs may move memory/storage closer to recurring-rev-like visibility vs historical spot cyclicality.
SNDK is viewed as most advanced structurally, with NBMs providing minimum contractual rev, financial guarantees, and upside participation through variable pricing.
NAND downside case appears better protected if contract floor pricing is locked near Q1 levels; Barclays sees SNDK CY28 EPS floor near $165 under flat price/cost assumptions.
MU may be next catalyst if mgmt provides more detail on financial hooks/prepayments in its 5yr SCA and future SCAs.
HDD pricing leverage may lag DRAM/NAND but improve into EoY as annual pricing contracts roll and 40TB mix increases.
STX/WDC LTAs and BTO model reduce inventory glut risk by locking demand visibility while allowing repricing through POs.
new_data: SNDK has 5 signed NBM contracts with financial guarantees exceeding $11B and minimum contractual rev of about $42B across 3 recent contracts.
SNDK recognized $400M of prepayment on balance sheet in FQ3.
MU announced its first 5yr Strategic Customer Agreement, though contract economics remain undisclosed.
Barclays estimates Mar qtr SNDK NAND $/GB +251% y/y and MU DRAM $/GB +113% y/y, vs WDC $/TB +9% y/y and STX mass-capacity $/TB +5% y/y.
STX nearline capacity is fully allocated through CY26, with CSP LTAs providing visibility through CY27 and demand growth projections into CY28.
WDC LTAs extend into CY29, with EB demand CAGR now trending toward the 30s% vs prior >25% into EoD.
management_insights: MU mgmt expects HBM to carry premium through-cycle margins vs the rest of the portfolio, eventually structurally higher.
STX mgmt indicates nearline capacity is fully allocated through CY26 and LTAs give visibility through CY27.
WDC mgmt indicated LTAs are paper contracts, with pricing/volume agreed in 1yr POs and penalties or higher pricing possible if customers alter volume needs.
WDC mgmt is not pursuing co-investment/prepayment structures, viewing HDD as lower-capex and structurally different from memory.
estimate_changes: SNDK upgraded EW to OW; PT raised to $2,300 from $1,200.
MU remains OW; PT raised to $1,175 from $675 on higher multiple, with rev/EPS unchanged.
STX remains OW; PT raised to $1,000 from $750 with higher rev/EPS and modest multiple expansion.
WDC remains OW; PT raised to $620 from $450 with higher rev/EPS and higher multiple.

estimate_change_details.note: MU PT increase from multiple expansion; rev/EPS unchanged.
SNDK PT increase reflects OW upgrade and higher multiple.
SNDK rev est raised.
SNDK EPS est raised.
SNDK rev est raised.
SNDK EPS est raised.
STX PT increase reflects higher CY27 EPS and multiple.
STX rev est raised.
STX EPS est raised.
STX rev est raised.
STX EPS est raised.
WDC PT increase reflects higher CY27 EPS and multiple.

evidence_quality: Company-reported results/guidance: primary evidence for SNDK NBMs, MU SCA, STX/WDC capacity commitments, and contract commentary.
Management meeting/call: primary but partly anecdotal evidence from WDC London mgmt mtg and recent earnings calls.
Market/industry data: triangulated pricing data from company reports and Barclays estimates across NAND, DRAM, and HDD.
Analyst model work: material portion of upside/downside and PT changes depends on Barclays assumptions for pricing floors, bit growth, margins, and multiples.
risks_or_watch_items: Memory or HDD oversupply could pressure ASPs and invalidate higher multiple thesis.
AI demand slowdown could reduce nearline/HBM demand and weaken pricing power.
STX HAMR/new-product ramp risk could limit pricing and GM upside.
WDC technology transition risk and worse-than-expected pricing remain key downside risks.
MU risk includes slower cost-per-bit declines and faster DRAM/NAND pricing erosion.

SNDK PT $2,300 from $1,200; upgraded to OW; CY27 EPS $230.10; CY26/CY27 rev $35.5B/$51.8B.

reports.title: HDD Update: Forecast Pricing Acceleration to Represent Upsides to Already High Bar of Buy-side Expectations; Still See Material Room for Both WDC and STX; Reiterate OW
reports.headline: JPM raises HDD ests/PTs on stronger pricing, durable AI-led demand, and disciplined capacity; reiterates OW on STX/WDC.
reports.summary: JPM raises HDD earnings forecasts for STX and WDC on a more pos view of pricing and incremental margins, arguing y/y HDD pricing gains should accel with sustained low- to mid-single-digit q/q increases. Demand remains robust from AI training/retraining, inferencing/agentic AI, and physical AI data creation, while disciplined unit capacity and longer LTAs support durable pricing. FY27 EPS outlook rises low-to-mid teens for both names, with Dec-26 PTs raised to $920 for STX and $650 for WDC; JPM sees WDC offering more upside due to lower valuation.
important_observations: HDD pricing is viewed as increasingly decoupled from historical cyclicality, with durable and accelerating conditions expected for multiple years.
Both STX and WDC reported y/y pricing increases together for the first time in the Mar qtr, and JPM expects the pace to accel in coming qtrs.
Low- to mid-single-digit q/q price increases are expected to sustain, supporting elevated q/q incremental margins.
AI storage demand is broadening beyond training/retraining into inferencing, agentic AI, robotics/autonomous systems, video/sensor data, and synthetic data generation.
Longer LTAs may improve supply assurance but are not expected to cap price increases, which JPM says are set more on a 1-year basis tied to order visibility.
WDC is preferred on upside potential, with 33% implied upside to JPM PT vs 13% for STX, despite both executing well.
new_data: JPM increased FY27 earnings outlook by low-to-mid teens for both WDC and STX.
Dec-26 PT raised to $650 from $530 for WDC.
Dec-26 PT raised to $920 from $775 for STX.
JPM estimates 90%+ earnings growth in FY27 and 55%+ earnings growth in FY28 for the HDD companies.
management_insights: Customers appear to be seeking multi-year supply assurance and visibility through potentially longer LTAs.
WDC and STX are maintaining disciplined capacity with no unit capacity expansion, supporting stronger pricing.
estimate_changes: FY27 earnings forecasts raised low-to-mid teens for WDC and STX, driven by higher pricing, rev, GM, and incremental margin assumptions.
WDC Dec-26 PT raised 23% to $650; OW reiterated.
STX Dec-26 PT raised 19% to $920; OW reiterated.

estimate_change_details.note: WDC PT raised on higher pricing, rev, GM, and earnings outlook.
STX PT raised on higher pricing, rev, GM, and earnings outlook.
JPM says earnings outlook raised low-to-mid teens for both WDC and STX, but exact prior/new EPS not provided in excerpt.

evidence_quality: Company-reported results/guidance: Primary evidence includes Mar qtr y/y pricing improvement for both HDD companies.
Management/customer indicators: LTA duration commentary and customer supply-assurance behavior support demand visibility but appear partly inferred.
Market/industry data: AI storage demand drivers and disciplined industry capacity provide macro/industry support for the thesis.
Analyst model work: PT and FY27 earnings revisions are JPM model-driven, with exact estimate tables not included in excerpt.
risks_or_watch_items: Buy-side expectations already high, so further upside depends on pricing accel exceeding a high bar.
Sustained q/q pricing increases are central to the thesis; decel would pressure rev/GM and incremental margin upside.
LTA structures could become a watch item if they start limiting pricing flexibility.
Capacity discipline by STX/WDC is key; any unit capacity expansion could weaken pricing durability.
AI-driven storage demand durability across training, inferencing, and physical AI remains a core LT debate.

WDC price: $490.09 as of 10 Jun 2026; mkt cap $188.7bn.
STX rating: OW, unchanged; PT $920 vs $775 prior.
WDC rating: OW, unchanged; PT $650 vs $530 prior.
Expected earnings growth: 90%+ in FY27 and 55%+ in FY28.
Expected sequential HDD pricing: low- to mid-single-digit q/q increases.

reports.title: Global PCs: Will RTX Spark Reinvigorate the PC Market?
reports.headline: MS cautious that NVDA's RTX Spark can revive PCs; high ASPs and limited vols likely keep impact small vs a declining CY26 PC TAM.
reports.summary: MS argues NVDA's RTX Spark is strategically important but unlikely to materially reinvigorate PC demand, with checks pointing to <10M cumulative units over 3 yrs vs ~270M annual PC TAM and device prices likely $1.8k-$3.5k. The note remains cautious on PCs, citing record memory/CPU-driven price hikes, weakening sell-through in elastic consumer/SMB and low-to-mid PC bands, and a view that local AI use cases are still not broad enough to accelerate replacement cycles.
important_observations: RTX Spark extends NVDA's CUDA/AI ecosystem into client PCs, but MS sees adoption constrained by pricing and limited NT volumes.
Prelim checks suggest cumulative RTX Spark PC shipments of <10M over 3 yrs, small relative to ~270M annual PC TAM.
High SoC ASP of ~$700-$900 implies end-device pricing of ~$1.8k-$3.5k, limiting mass-market adoption.
PC demand appears to be rolling over as price-sensitive consumer/SMB and low-to-mid-range segments push back on record price hikes.
Win 11 upgrades still appear to be a support factor, but not enough to offset broader demand softness in MS's view.
new_data: NVDA announced RTX Spark, an Arm-based PC SoC combining Grace CPU, Blackwell GPU, AI acceleration, and unified memory.
OEM partners named include Dell, HP, Lenovo, ASUS, Acer, MSI, and MSFT; NVDA will continue selling developer-focused DGX systems directly.
Higher-end N1X systems expected in fall, with lower-end N1 to follow later.
MS checks indicate declining y/y sell-through at some PC OEMs in elastic customer and price bands.
management_insights: NVDA frames Agentic AI and local inference as drivers of a new class of high-performance AI PCs and a broader client TAM.
evidence_quality: Channel checks: Primary/anecdotal checks support <10M cumulative RTX Spark shipment expectation and signs of weaker sell-through in elastic PC segments.
Company-reported product announcement: NVDA's RTX Spark platform details and OEM partner list are based on GTC/Computex announcements.
Market/industry data: PC TAM cited at ~270M units annually and CY26 global PC TAM expected down 10%+ y/y.
Analyst inference: Conclusion that RTX Spark will not revive PC demand relies on MS model work around ASPs, device pricing, replacement cycles, and AI-PC use-case maturity.
risks_or_watch_items: Broader-than-expected local AI use cases could pull forward replacement cycles.
OEM adoption, pricing, and availability of N1X/N1 systems in fall are key checks.
Consumer/SMB sell-through and low-to-mid PC demand need monitoring as price hikes flow through.
Memory/CPU price inflation could further pressure unit demand.
key_numbers: CY26 global PC TAM expected down 10%+ y/y
RTX Spark cumulative shipment checks: <10M units over 3 yrs

reports.title: Americas Technology: Internet & Interactive Entertainment: 10 Industry Themes for 2026 & Focus Stocks Going Forward
reports.file_name: Americas_ Technology_ Internet & Interactive Entertainment_ 10 Industry Themes for 2026 & Focus Stocks Going Forward (Mid-Yea.pdf
reports.headline: GS mid-year internet/interactive entertainment outlook frames 2026 around AI capex/opex, global consumer health, and AI disruption risk
reports.summary: GS refreshes its 2026 internet and interactive entertainment themes at mid-year, arguing investor focus has narrowed to three overlapping debates: the AI investment cycle and capital needs, soft/mixed global consumer datapoints ex-CN, and AI disruption risk to terminal values across marketplaces, lead gen, and content businesses. The note remains constructive that AI adoption is still early in enterprise workflows while consumer usage is moving faster but largely unmonetized, leaving monetization, ROI visibility, and traffic/commerce disintermediation as key NT/MT tracking items.
important_observations: Investor breadth and exposure across the coverage group have narrowed, reflected in weaker YTD performance for most names in coverage.
AI investment remains in an upcycle, with focus on capex/opex scale-up, external capital needs, and visibility into returns on spend.
Enterprise AI adoption is still early, constrained by high token prices and slower workflow change, while consumer adoption is faster but mostly unmonetized.
Global consumer datapoints ex-CN are mixed/softening, with inflation pressure and rising competition for wallet share across advertising, commerce, and offline/online channels.
AI disruption risk is increasingly being capitalized into terminal-value debates for marketplaces, lead gen, content creation/distribution, and businesses exposed to agentic commerce.
new_data: Mid-year 2026 thematic refresh updates GS's Dec 2025 year-ahead preview and companion slide deck.
Post-1Q26 earnings review informs the framing that investor debates have consolidated around AI spend, consumer health, and AI disruption.
YTD stock performance across most covered names is cited as evidence of narrower investor breadth.
evidence_quality: Analyst model work/theme work: primarily GS synthesis of coverage trends and valuation debates; useful for framing investor perception but not company-specific primary data.
Company-reported results/guidance: post-1Q26 earnings review is referenced as an input, but the excerpt does not provide co-specific results or guide details.
Market/industry data: YTD stock performance and investor exposure/breadth are cited directionally; evidence is market-based but not quantified in the provided text.
Analyst inference: views on enterprise adoption constraints, consumer AI monetization, and terminal-value disruption are mostly interpretive and should be weighted as thematic judgment.
risks_or_watch_items: AI ROI visibility and the capital required to fund rising capex/opex cycles.
Pace of enterprise workflow adoption as token costs and implementation friction evolve.
Timing and format of consumer AI monetization through ads, commerce, or other models.
Evidence of agentic commerce shifting traffic, queries, lead gen, or marketplace take rates.
Further weakening in global consumer demand ex-CN and competition for consumer spend.
key_numbers: Coverage performance datapoint: most covered names weaker YTD

reports.title: Americas Technology: Hardware - Traditional & Accelerated Servers: An Industry Primer
reports.file_name: Americas Technology_ Hardware_ Traditional & accelerated servers_ An industry primer.pdf
reports.companies_covered: DELL, HPE, SMCI, IBM, NVDA, PENG
reports.headline: Server industry primer frames AI-driven accelerated servers as the dominant growth pool while memory inflation and consolidation pressure reshape traditional server economics.
reports.summary: GS presents a server industry primer using 650 Group and company data, forecasting the total server market to grow at a +38% 5-yr CAGR from ~$284bn in 2025 to ~$1.4tn by 2030, led by accelerated servers at ~$1.2tn. Key debates include whether traditional server refreshes can offset unit consolidation from denser platforms, how agentic AI may increase CPU attach demand, whether branded OEMs can defend share and margin vs ODMs/white box, and how structural DRAM/NAND inflation could force major server ASP increases or margin pressure.
important_observations: Traditional server demand has a refresh tailwind from an aged installed base, but densification creates a unit headwind if next-gen systems replace legacy fleets at 3:1 to 7:1 ratios.
Agentic AI could increase the CPU role in AI infra, with CPU-to-GPU attach moving closer to 1:2 or 1:1 vs 1:4 to 1:8 in traditional training clusters.
Hyperscaler growth has structurally shifted share toward ODMs/white box; branded OEMs remain strongest in enterprise and tier 2/neocloud where services, support, and integration matter.
AI server profit pools are large but hardware GM is lower than traditional servers because GPUs dominate BOM and leave less value capture for OEMs.
Memory inflation is a major traditional-server risk: DRAM/NAND could move from ~30% of BOM historically to >60% by end-CY26 absent offsets.
Services attach is a key margin lever for OEMs, especially in enterprise AI and traditional servers.
new_data: 650 Group forecasts total server rev at ~$1.4tn by 2030, a +38% 5-yr CAGR.
Traditional servers were ~$89bn in 2025 rev, +10% y/y, with ~8.4mn units, -18% y/y, and ASP ~$10.5k, +35% y/y.
Accelerated servers were ~$195bn in 2025 rev, +69% y/y, with ~1.9mn units, +27% y/y, and ASP ~$100k, +33% y/y.
GS estimates ~70mn traditional servers in the global installed base with avg life of 5.7 yrs; 32% are 5+ yrs old vs 8% a decade ago.
White box represented ~23% of traditional server units and ~28% of rev in 2025 vs 15% and 8% in 2014.
Hyperscalers represented 82% of 2025 AI server shipments; enterprise only ~5%.
management_insights: DELL reported most of its traditional server installed base was 14G or older, supporting a refresh opportunity.
DELL has cited 17G consolidation ratios of 3:1 vs 15G and 7:1 vs 14G, creating both ASP/margin opportunity and unit risk.
HPE has reported one Gen12 server can replace 7 Gen11 or 14 Gen10 servers while reducing power by 65%.
Industry OEMs are pursuing LTAs with memory suppliers that include more binding commitments, prepayments, penalty clauses, and floor-price structures.
evidence_quality: Market/industry data: primarily based on 650 Group server market forecasts and share data, supplemented by IDC, Gartner, Census Bureau, Epoch, and Lawrence Berkeley National Lab.
Company-reported data: DELL, HPE, hyperscaler depreciation/useful-life disclosures, and vendor product specs support installed-base, consolidation, and refresh analysis.
Channel checks: GS cites checks indicating Dell program-level services are viewed as best-in-class and that memory LTAs are becoming more binding.
Analyst model work: BOM, installed-base, scenario analysis, margin impact, and profit-pool sensitivity are mostly GS inference and should be treated as directional.
risks_or_watch_items: DRAM/NAND price inflation could exceed OEM pass-through ability, pressuring GM or forcing demand-dampening ASP hikes.
Server consolidation may shrink future traditional server unit demand even if compute demand grows.
White box/ODM share gains could pressure branded OEM share and pricing, especially as sophisticated neoclouds shift toward custom designs.
Enterprise AI server adoption remains nascent, making timing and scale of OEM upside uncertain.
AI server economics remain highly dependent on GPU allocation, pricing, and NVDA ecosystem dynamics.
key_numbers: Total server market: ~$284bn rev in 2025, +45% y/y; forecast ~$1.4tn by 2030.
Total server market forecast: +38% 5-yr CAGR through 2030.
Traditional server market forecast: ~$164bn by 2030, +13% 5-yr CAGR.
Accelerated server market forecast: ~$1.2tn by 2030, +45% 5-yr CAGR.
Traditional server 2025: ~$89bn rev, +10% y/y; ~8.4mn units, -18%; ASP ~$10.5k, +35%.
Accelerated server 2025: ~$195bn rev, +69% y/y; ~1.9mn units, +27%; ASP ~$100k, +33%.
Traditional server installed base: ~70mn units; avg life 5.7 yrs; 32% aged 5+ yrs.
Top 5 traditional server vendors: unit share fell to 42% in 2025 from 53% in 2014; rev share fell to 57% from 63%.
Hyperscaler traditional server market: 72% addressed by ODM/white box in 2025; enterprise market 98% addressed by branded OEMs.
Traditional server hardware margins: branded vendors typically ~15-20% GM and ~6-8% OM.

reports.title: Alphabet Inc.: Unpacking The AI Transition In Search
reports.headline: Barclays sees AI-driven Search changes as likely rev-accretive via query growth, commercial mix and CTR, but expects 2H26 Search decel and Apple/Siri access-point risk to complicate the narrative.
reports.summary: Barclays argues GOOGL's AI Search transition is more likely a monetization tailwind than a rev airpocket, with AI Overviews/AI Mode supporting query growth, AI Max/PMax lifting commercial query mix, and new AI ad formats potentially extending CTR gains that have historically driven most Search rev growth. The NT debate is less about current Search monetization and more about market perception as Search growth decels from 1Q26's 19% y/y toward 14% in 2H26 amid tougher ad comps/FX, just as OpenAI ads, Apple Intelligence/Siri 2.0, agentic apps and AI hardware launch. Barclays remains OW, PT $405, and flags Apple devices/Safari as the biggest structural access-point risk given they represent nearly half of queries and over half of Search rev.
important_observations: CTR expansion has been the largest driver of Google Search rev over the past decade, more important than commercial query or CPC growth; Barclays views AI Search layouts as potentially supportive of further CTR gains.
AI Overviews/AI Mode appear to have reversed weak mature-market query trends, with Barclays estimating total queries grow ~7% in 2025-26E before moderating.
AI Max may turn commercial query mix upward after a decade-long decline, while PMax gives GOOGL a mechanism to monetize new surfaces quickly across O+O properties.
Safari toolbar is the key risk area: ~30% of total queries and ~38% of Search rev exposure, with fewer new AI Search features than Chrome/GSA and greater Apple control over future AI access points.
Street may conflate expected 2H26 Search rev decel from comps/FX with AI competition, creating share risk even if underlying Search product trends remain healthy.
new_data: GOOGL Search rev modeled at $225B in 2025, $260B in 2026E, $286B in 2027E and $308B in 2028E.
Total Search queries modeled at 5.2T in 2025, 5.5T in 2026E, 5.8T in 2027E and 6.1T in 2028E.
Apple devices estimated at just under 50% of queries; Safari alone ~30% of queries and 36-38% of Search rev in 2025-27E.
Safari rev exposure estimated at $84B in 2025, $97B in 2026E and $102B in 2027E.
management_insights: Mgmt has said AI Overviews/AI Mode are driving more Search frequency and engagement.
Mgmt commentary suggests both overall and commercial queries have been growing for several years, contrasting with weaker tone during the 2018-19 Search ad transition.
Recent call commentary emphasized Gemini deployment across ads infrastructure, better intent understanding, ads quality and new AI user experiences.
Barclays reads current mgmt communication as consistent with healthy Search backdrop and no sign of a 2019-style monetization issue.

estimate_change_details.note: PT unchanged from 30-Apr-2026; note focuses on AI Search framework rather than estimate revisions.

reports.title: 'Cleaning the Lens' for Optical Investors
reports.companies_covered: IIVI, FNSR, CIEN, OCLR, LITE, INFN, ACIA
reports.headline: Optical sector scorecard favors IIVI, turns cautious on ACIA; 3D sensing/telecom/industrial preferred, datacomm components least attractive.
reports.summary: Morgan Stanley introduces a proprietary optical scorecard across 7 names, initiating IIVI at OW, FNSR/OCLR at EW, and downgrading ACIA to UW. The core call is that optical has strong secular demand but poor visibility, fragmented supply, and cycle-driven volatility, so stock selection should be based on theme exposure plus valuation. MS favors 3D sensing, telecom ex-China, and industrial/semicap; is neutral on China and datacomm systems; and is cautious on datacomm components due to oversupply, pricing pressure, and hyperscale buyer power.
important_observations: IIVI ranks best overall due to diversified industrial/semicap, telecom, and 3D sensing exposure, with less volatile earnings power than optical peers.
ACIA screens worst: high datacomm/China exposure, high valuation, customer concentration, and Street FY19 ests viewed as too high.
3D sensing is the top 2018 optical theme, driven by Apple expansion beyond iPhone X plus Android adoption, with supply tight through most of the year.
Datacomm components face margin pressure from fierce competition, shorter product cycles, hyperscale pricing pressure, and new entrants.
Strategic activity/consolidation is framed as a major LT positive catalyst because supplier fragmentation is depressing margins vs concentrated buyers.
new_data: New optical scorecard ranks IIVI #1, OCLR #2, CIEN #3, FNSR #4, LITE #5, INFN #6, ACIA #7 overall.
MS estimates 3D sensing demand could rise 300-500% in 2018 as adoption expands across Apple and some Android devices.

Android 3D sensing VCSEL demand ests: 44mm in 2018e, 153mm in 2019e, 510mm in 2020e.
China represents 25-30% of global optical demand, with 2017 inventory correction expected to abate starting 2Q18.
management_insights: IIVI mgmt targets EPS growth 1.5-2.0x rev growth, with rev targeted at 2.5-4.0x GDP, helped by 3D sensing and silicon carbide investments.
INFN mgmt mtg suggested multiple new customers are trialing refreshed products, with traction beyond long haul but rev impact more likely late 2018/early 2019.
FNSR recently noted it would discontinue certain delayed ROADM projects, limiting NT upside despite 3D sensing/ROADM potential.
ACIA mgmt expectations imply no 2018 growth, while Street assumes a strong 2019 rebound that MS views as aggressive.

reports.title: AAOI: Initiate at Strong Buy - Hard-Nosed Economics and Timid Estimates
reports.headline: Initiates AAOI at Strong Buy with $85 PT on 25G/100G data-center optical upgrade, low-cost vertical integration, and upside to rev/GM ests.
reports.summary: Needham initiates AAOI at Strong Buy with an $85 PT, arguing Street ests are too timid as 25G/100G Data Comm demand hits an inflection in CY2Q17. The bull case rests on AAOI doubling laser capacity toward 1mm/month by EoY, mix shifting from 10G/40G to higher-ASP/higher-GM 100G, and vertical integration giving AAOI a cost/yield edge as supply tightens through CY17 and potentially balances in CY18. The note explicitly models CY18 pricing pressure but still sees robust growth, resilient GM, share gains, and valuation upside.
important_observations: AAOI capacity ramp from ~400k lasers/month at start of CY17 to 1mm/month by EoY implies rev growth materially above Street’s 26% CY1Q-to-CY4Q increase, before ASP mix benefit.
100G mix expected to rise from ~30% of Data Comm sales to 50-55% by EoY, with 100G GM estimated 50-55% vs 40G at 30-35%.
Analyst sees CY2Q17 as the 25G/100G inflection point as NIC card and QSFP28 100G optics supply constraints ease.
Vertical integration, line fungibility, and 90%+ yields are framed as structural advantages, potentially adding 15-20 pts of cost advantage vs peers forced to build new lines.
CY18 supply/demand balance is the main debate; model assumes pricing pressure in CY1Q18, but analyst assigns 40-50% odds demand remains above supply through CY18.
Customer concentration is material: top 10 customers were 95.5% of CY16 rev and 96.7% of CY1Q17 rev; Amazon was 54.6% of CY16 rev and Microsoft 18.3%.
new_data: Initiation includes CY17E rev of $448mm and EPS of $4.60; CY18E rev of $528mm and EPS of $5.03.
Initial PT set at $85, based on 3.0x EV/Sales and 16.5x EV/E.
AAOI expected to exit CY17 with capacity for ~3mm transceivers annually vs ~1.2mm at start of year.
100G ASP cited as at least 40-50% higher than 40G.
Ovum/Dell Oro cited for ~250% 100G Data Comm port growth in CY17 and ~150% in CY18.
One scaled-out DC estimated to require ~1.3mm 100G ports.
management_insights: Mgmt/AAOI claims laser costs are ~30% below next-best competitor and 40-50% below marginal players.
Mgmt/AAOI believes all-in transceiver costs are ~20% below nearest competitor and 30%+ below marginal competitor in target segments.
Mgmt/AAOI argues 10G/40G lines can be reused for 25G/100G, reducing capex, depreciation, and yield-ramp drag vs competitors.
Mgmt/AAOI sees 5G RAN and 10G PON as LT optionality, with 5G deployments likely starting CY19/CY20.
Mgmt/AAOI positions CATV/DOCSIS 3.1 as supportive but not the core investment story.
estimate_changes: Initiation of coverage; no prior Needham ests shown for rev/EPS.
Initial Strong Buy rating and $85 PT.
CY17/CY18 ests explicitly bake in CY18 pricing pressure and modest GM pressure.

reports.title: F3Q26: ARR miss on deal slippage, Fully Ramped ARR commentary a bright spot
reports.headline: GWRE F3Q26 ARR miss drove AH selloff, but FRARR guide/commentary supports MT upside case; Buy, $225 PT.
reports.summary: GS remains Buy on GWRE despite a -14% AH reaction after F3Q26, driven mainly by a modest ARR miss after several qtrs of outperformance and mgmt's explanation of deal slippage into F4Q. The note frames F4Q as a key “show me” catalyst for restoring deal-visibility confidence, while highlighting stronger-than-expected FY26 FRARR commentary and a developing AI monetization path as potential drivers of MT upward est revisions.

GS views AI ARR contribution as more likely a CY27/CY28 narrative than CY26 given timing/magnitude opacity.

reports.title: AI Capex 2.0: If You Build It, They Will Finance It - Updated Growth & Capital Markets Forecasts
reports.companies_covered: GOOGL, AMZN, META, MSFT, ORCL, NVDA, AMD, AVGO
reports.headline: JPM raises AI infra capex and financing forecasts as compute scarcity, agentic inference, and high LTC project funding drive a multi-trillion-dollar capital markets cycle.
reports.summary: JPM frames AI infra as a structural capital markets event, raising its 2030 data center growth fcst to 138 GW from 122 GW and total AI capex to $5.5T from $5.1T, with debt financing now expected at $4.1T. The note argues power remains the key constraint, but BYO-power, behind-the-meter deals, ASIC efficiency, and higher compute per MW modestly debottleneck growth. JPM remains bullish across AI-linked credit, especially wider-spread project deals that can refi or migrate to IG, while highlighting that GPU/ASIC financing could become the bigger funding challenge as silicon capex rises into 2030.
important_observations: AI/data center issuance has already exceeded $300B since JPM's Nov note, with debt markets becoming a structural funding source rather than episodic support.
Agentic inference is now the marginal driver of compute demand; usage growth is overwhelming 60-70% annual per-token cost declines.
Power remains the binding constraint, but developers are using BYO-power, behind-the-meter structures, better GPUs, and ASICs to extract more compute per MW.
JPM expects HG corporate markets to be the main funding workhorse, contributing >$2.1T over five years, with ~$350B from leveraged finance.
GPU/custom ASIC financings are expected to exceed $3T over five years and continue growing into 2030 as replacement demand emerges.
Hyperscalers remain cash-generative, but capex is outpacing OCF; external debt, equity, FX issuance, project finance, and SPVs are becoming recurring tools.
JPM sees compute scarcity supporting AI-linked credit for now, but warns adoption could stall if user costs remain too high before capacity additions reduce token costs.
new_data: AI-related debt issuance has exceeded $300B since Nov, with data center debt a major driver of near-record issuance.
JPM now forecasts 138 GW of capacity growth through 2030 vs 122 GW previously.
Total AI capex through 2030 raised to $5.5T from $5.1T; assumed debt financing component raised to $4.1T.
Top 4 US hyperscalers guided to ~$700-725B of 2026 capex, up ~75% y/y from ~$410B in 2025.
OpenAI annualized rev run-rate cited above $25B as of Mar 2026; ChatGPT weekly active users >900M.
Anthropic run-rate rev described as approaching $50B, up from ~$9B at YE2025.
management_insights: Hyperscaler mgmt teams appear to be preserving optionality by using high-LTC project financing now, leaving OCF/equity proceeds available if future rates or markets worsen.
GOOGL mgmt increased FY26 capex guide by $10B q/q to $185B midpoint and expects FY27 capex to rise significantly vs 2026.
AMZN mgmt points to Trainium as a way to save tens of billions in annual capex and improve margins vs merchant GPU reliance.
META mgmt continues prioritizing model quality and product scale before monetization, leaving ROIC timing less clear.
MSFT mgmt expects capacity constraints to persist through at least CY26 and has lifted spending expectations due partly to component inflation.

reports.headline: UBS sees AVGO AI rev step-up potential into C2028 from OpenAI/Anthropic SPV, but flags Google TPU roadmap/supply uncertainty as key swing factor.
reports.summary: UBS addresses investor questions post-earnings, arguing AVGO's 20GW SPV with Apollo/Blackstone formalizes financing/capacity for already-modeled OpenAI and Anthropic engagements in C2027, while implying much larger C2028 upside if shipments scale toward ~13GW. OpenAI/Anthropic readiness to deploy owned capacity, including powered shell availability and full software stack/orchestration, is a major NT/MT execution gating factor.

reports.title: May 2026 Results: Strong Underwriting Results Address Key Bearish Thesis
reports.headline: May results showed much stronger underwriting than MS expected, weakening the bear thesis around CR deterioration and growth decel, though MS remains UW with $190 PT.
reports.summary: PGR's May 2026 results were well ahead of MS ests, with op EPS of $2.18 vs $1.36, total CR of 82.1% vs 90.9%, and NWP growth of 5.9% vs 4.6%. The upside was driven by better core loss ratios, low CATs, favorable PYD, and strong segment-level underwriting across personal auto, property, and commercial lines. PIF growth is still decel y/y, especially personal auto adds, but came in better than MS expected, reducing NT concern that softer market competition is quickly eroding profitability and growth.
important_observations: May results directly challenged the bearish thesis that soft-market competition would drive underwriting deterioration and sharper growth slowdown.
Personal auto PIF adds of 134K were down 45.5% y/y but 41% above MS ests, suggesting growth decel is less severe than feared.
Core underwriting remained strong: total core CR was 83.7%, 390 bps better than MS est, with favorable loss ratios across major segments.
Low CATs and favorable PYD materially helped results, so investors should separate sustainable core profitability from one-time/volatile loss items.
MS argues valuation could rebound if PGR can show a path to stable underwriting profit despite slower growth.

reports.headline: PGR May op EPS beat on materially better loss ratio, favorable PYD, and lower CAT load; PIF adds light vs JEF.
reports.summary: Jefferies says PGR May op EPS of $2.18 beat its $1.28 est, driven by a much better reported CR of 82.1% vs JEF 90.8%, with the upside from AYLR ex-CAT, 3.1 pts favorable PYD, and CAT losses below fcst. PIF growth was a mild neg, with +203k MoM net adds below JEF's ~250k, while NII and buybacks were better than expected. Jefferies expected PGR shares to outperform on the LR beat, while maintaining Hold and $213 PT.
important_observations: Underlying loss ratio was the key positive: AYLR ex-CAT of 64.0% beat JEF by 277 bps and improved 126 bps y/y.
Favorable PYD of 3.1 pts was a major source of upside vs JEF's 0% assumption, raising the debate over sustainability of reserve releases.
CAT LR of 1.4 pts was far better than JEF's 4.2 pts, especially in Property where CAT load was only 3.4 pts vs 30.0% est.
PIF adds were below expectations despite solid Personal Auto MoM growth, leaving growth vs margin as the key NT watch item.
Repurchases of $252mn were among the highest monthly BB levels in recent years, adding a capital-return positive.
